message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://bmcneurosci.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2202-14-74
**Title:** Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar — an fMRI study (BMC Neuroscience, 2013)
**Found via:** formant-synth-vs-neural/blind-power-users search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

It focuses entirely on integrating chemical ontologies with biological databases—specifically aligning the Gene Ontology with the ChEBI chemical ontology.

To address your research question about ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects, you would need to locate neuroscience or cognitive psychology literature specifically examining auditory processing, cross-modal plasticity, or speech perception studies—not ontology development literature.

Links: [{"title":"Network Modeling for Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) Signals during Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension in Late-Blind Listeners | PLOS One","url":"https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0132196"},{"title":"V10. Learning to comprehend time-compressed speech affects premotor and cerebellar regions in late-blind and normally sighted subjects | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/279737259_V10_Learning_to_comprehend_time-compressed_speech_affects_premotor_and_cerebellar_regions_in_late-blind_and_normally_sighted_subjects"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar - a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23879896/"},{"title":"Frontiers | Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans","url":"https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/human-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3847124/"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3847124/"},{"title":"Frontiers | How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects?","url":"https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/psychology/articles/10.3389/fpsyg.2013.00530/full"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study | BMC Neuroscience | Full Text","url":"https://bmcneurosci.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2202-14-74"}]

I found the study you were searching for. The paper is titled "Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study" by authors Susanne Dietrich, Ingo Hertrich, and Hermann Ackermann, published in 2013.

The study found that individuals with vision loss can learn to understand spoken language at rates of up to about 22 syllables per second, far exceeding the maximum performance level of normal-sighted listeners (approximately 8 syl/s).

In their fMRI findings, blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar (Pv), in addition to other regions like the left inferior frontal gyrus, bilateral posterior superior temporal sulcus, and left supplementary motor area.

The full text of the study is available through the BMC Neuroscience journal website and the NIH/PubMed Central repositories.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: The correct PMC ID is PMC3847124. Let me fetch the full text from PMC.

input.prompt: Extract key findings about ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects. Specifically: (1) What speaking rates (syllables per second) were tested, and the maximum rate blind subjects could comprehend vs normal-sighted listeners? Give exact numbers. (2) Which brain regions were engaged during ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects (primary visual cortex / V1, fusiform gyrus, pulvinar, etc.)? (3) Any quantitative comprehension/intelligibility data, thresholds, accuracy figures, or correlations between brain activation and comprehension. (4) The publication date. (5) Whether this is original peer-reviewed research. Provide direct verbatim quotes for each finding.

content.content:
# Key Findings: Ultra-fast Speech Comprehension in Blind Subjects

## (1) Speaking Rates and Comprehension

**Tested rates:** The study examined moderately fast speech at 8 syllables per second (syl/s) and ultra-fast speech at 16 syl/s.

**Blind subjects' capability:** "blind people...may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second"

**Sighted controls:** "exceeding by far the maximum performance level of normal-sighted listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)"

## (2) Brain Regions Engaged During Ultra-fast Speech

The study identified multiple activated areas in blind subjects with high ultra-fast comprehension:

"blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar (Pv)"

Additional regions included: left inferior frontal gyrus, bilateral posterior superior temporal sulcus, and left supplementary motor area.

## (3) Quantitative Comprehension Data

**Ultra-fast speech performance (blind subjects):** "extended...from 0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, _SD_ ± 24.0%)"

**Sighted controls:** "performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, _SD_ ± 4.7%)"

**Correlation finding:** "all ROIs showed a significant positive trend towards stronger hemodynamic activation under the ultra-fast speech condition in case of enhanced ultra-fast speech perception capabilities (V1: _r_ = .533, _p_ < .05)"

## (4) Publication Date

July 23, 2013 (BMC Neuroscience)

## (5) Research Type

Original peer-reviewed research published in an open-access journal with ethical approval and informed consent procedures documented.

content.text: I have the full text now. The source is the canonical primary research article. Let me record the structured findings.

claims.claim: Highly trained blind listeners can comprehend speech at rates up to ~22 syllables/second, roughly 2.5x to 3x the maximum rate normal-sighted listeners can follow (~8 syl/s). This validates that extreme speed-listening (the target of an aggressively speed-optimized track) is a learnable, real perceptual capability concentrated in a trained power-listener population.
claims.quote: blind people...may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second...exceeding by far the maximum performance level of normal-sighted listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)

claims.claim: At 16 syllables/second (ultra-fast condition), blind subjects' comprehension ranged from 0 to 93% (mean 59.7%, SD 24.0%), whereas sighted controls consistently fell below 16% (mean 9.0%, SD 4.7%). This quantifies a massive trained-listener vs untrained gap at ultra-fast rates and confirms ~16 syl/s as a meaningful design ceiling distinguishing the two intended use cases (general students vs trained power listeners).
claims.quote: extended...from 0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, SD ± 24.0%)... performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, SD ± 4.7%)

claims.claim: Ultra-fast speech comprehension in proficient blind listeners engages non-auditory brain regions, specifically right primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus, and bilateral pulvinar, alongside classic language regions (left inferior frontal gyrus, posterior superior temporal sulcus, supplementary motor area). This indicates the ultra-fast skill relies on cross-modal neural recruitment from training/plasticity rather than just acoustic clarity of the signal.
claims.quote: blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar (Pv)

claims.claim: Activation of recruited regions (including V1, r = .533, p < .05) correlated positively with individual ultra-fast comprehension ability, meaning the capacity scales with listener-specific neural adaptation. This implies comprehensibility at extreme speed is substantially listener-trained rather than a fixed property of the TTS signal, bounding how much a speed-optimized voice alone can deliver to untrained users.
claims.quote: all ROIs showed a significant positive trend towards stronger hemodynamic activation under the ultra-fast speech condition in case of enhanced ultra-fast speech perception capabilities (V1: r = .533, p < .05)

content.text: Extracted findings from the primary research article (Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann, BMC Neuroscience 2013, open-access full text at https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3847124/). Source quality is primary (original peer-reviewed fMRI study). Four falsifiable claims recorded covering the ~22 syl/s trained ceiling, the 16 syl/s blind-vs-sighted comprehension gap (59.7% vs 9.0%), the cross-modal brain regions engaged, and the activation-comprehension correlation. The central relevance to the research question is that it bounds the speed-optimized "power listener" track as a trainable, listener-dependent capability rather than a property the TTS signal alone can confer.
